skill.name: localhero
skill.description: Manages i18n translations with Localhero.ai. Use when working with translation files, adding user-facing strings, or modifying UI copy.
skill.summary: AI-powered i18n translation management for projects using Localhero.ai. Writes source strings and integrates with translation workflows.
skill.author: localheroai

security_audit.summary: This skill is a legitimate i18n translation management tool. All 75 static analysis detections are false positives. The detected 'shell backtick execution' patterns are markdown code blocks documenting CLI usage, not actual code execution. The 'credential access' references are documentation about API key configuration. The 'network' detections are legitimate URLs to the Localhero.ai service website. The skill uses the standard allowed-tools directive to run the Localhero CLI for translation management only.

dangerous_patterns.title: Documentation Shell Commands (False Positive)
dangerous_patterns.description: Static analyzer flagged markdown code blocks showing CLI examples (npx @localheroai/cli translate). These are legitimate documentation examples in SKILL.md, cli-reference.md, and README.md, not actual code execution. The skill is designed to help developers work with the Localhero CLI for i18n management.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: All flagged locations are markdown code blocks containing CLI examples, not executable code. The skill correctly uses allowed-tools directive for legitimate npx command execution.

dangerous_patterns.title: API Key Documentation (False Positive)
dangerous_patterns.description: Static analyzer flagged LOCALHERO_API_KEY references as 'credential access'. These are legitimate documentation showing users how to configure authentication for the Localhero CLI, not credential exfiltration.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: API key references appear only in documentation context explaining how to authenticate with the Localhero service. No credential exfiltration behavior detected.

dangerous_patterns.title: Hardcoded URLs (False Positive)
dangerous_patterns.description: Static analyzer flagged URLs to localhero.ai and github.com as 'hardcoded URLs'. These are legitimate service endpoints and documentation links.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: All URLs point to legitimate Localhero.ai service endpoints and official documentation. No data exfiltration endpoints detected.

content.value_statement: Managing multi-language translations across large projects is tedious and error-prone. This skill helps AI coding assistants write source strings correctly, follow project naming conventions, and integrate with Localhero.ai translation workflows.
actual_capabilities: Writes source language i18n strings following project conventions
Reads glossary terms and project settings for consistent terminology
Adds or modifies translation keys in JSON, YAML, or PO/POT files
Explains i18n file structure and key naming patterns
Guides users to Localhero.ai web UI for translation review
limitations: Only writes source language strings, does not directly edit target translations
Requires Localhero.ai account and CLI authentication to function
Cannot access project glossary or settings without running CLI commands

use_cases.title: Adding new UI strings to a multi-language app
use_cases.description: Developer needs to add a new feature with user-facing text. The skill examines existing keys, follows naming patterns, and writes correct source strings.
use_cases.target_user: Frontend developers working on localized applications

use_cases.title: Fixing inconsistent terminology across translations
use_cases.description: Developer wants to ensure product names and technical terms match the glossary. The skill loads glossary terms and suggests correct usage.

use_cases.title: Setting up i18n for a new project
use_cases.description: New project needs i18n infrastructure. The skill explains how to initialize Localhero, configure locales, and follow best practices for key naming.
use_cases.target_user: Developers starting internationalization on new projects

prompt_templates.title: Add new translation keys
prompt_templates.prompt: Add translation keys for the new onboarding flow. The screen has a welcome heading, two paragraphs of explanatory text, and three buttons: 'Get Started', 'Learn More', and 'Skip'.

prompt_templates.title: Follow existing naming conventions
prompt_templates.prompt: We need to add keys for the user profile settings page. Check the existing source files first to understand our naming pattern, then add keys for the page title, section headings, and form labels.

prompt_templates.title: Handle gettext format correctly
prompt_templates.prompt: This project uses gettext PO files. We added a new error message: 'The file could not be saved because the disk is full'. Add this as a msgid with appropriate msgctxt for context.

prompt_templates.title: Integrate with translation workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the localhero.json configuration and our source translation file. Check if we have a GitHub workflow set up for automatic translations, and if not, run the CLI to generate translations for our new keys.

output_examples.input: Add a translation key for 'Welcome back, {name}' where name is the user's display name
output_examples.output:
Based on the existing naming pattern (users.* for user-related strings), I would add:

JSON format: "users.welcome_back": "Welcome back, {name}"

This follows your project's dot-separated namespace convention and uses the standard {name} placeholder format.

output_examples.input: How do I change the tone for product names in translations?
output_examples.output:
To adjust tone, style, or terminology for product names, use the Localhero.ai web UI:

1. Go to https://localhero.ai and open your project
2. Navigate to Settings or Glossary
3. Add or update glossary terms for product names
4. The glossary terms will be used consistently in future translations

You can also run `npx @localheroai/cli glossary --output json` to see current glossary terms.

output_examples.input: Our checkout flow has new error messages. Help us add them to the translations.
output_examples.output:
I'll check your existing translation structure first, then add the new error message keys following your naming pattern.

For checkout errors, your project uses the pattern: errors.checkout.<specific_error>

New key to add: "errors.checkout.payment_declined": "Your payment was declined. Please try a different payment method."

This keeps all checkout errors grouped under the errors.checkout namespace for easy maintenance.

best_practices: Run `npx @localheroai/cli glossary --output json` before writing user-facing strings to ensure terminology consistency
Examine existing source files first to match the project's key naming pattern (JSON dot notation, YAML nested, or gettext msgid)
Group related keys by feature or page (e.g., dashboard.*, checkout.*) for easier maintenance and discovery
anti_patterns: Do not write target language translations directly - only write source language strings and let Localhero.ai handle translations
Avoid generic key names like 'text1' or 'label2' - use descriptive names that indicate content purpose like 'checkout.submit_button'
Do not skip the glossary check - inconsistent terminology across languages frustrates users and complicates translation updates

faq.question: Does this skill translate my strings automatically?
faq.answer: No. This skill only writes source language strings. Translations to other languages are handled by Localhero.ai, either through their GitHub Action on PRs or by running the translate CLI command.

faq.question: What i18n file formats does this skill support?
faq.answer: The skill works with JSON, YAML, and PO/POT (gettext) formats. It reads existing files to understand your project's naming conventions and follows the same patterns when adding new keys.

faq.question: How do I authenticate with Localhero.ai?
faq.answer: Run `npx @localheroai/cli login` for interactive authentication, or use `npx @localheroai/cli login --api-key YOUR_KEY` with your API key from https://localhero.ai/api-keys

faq.question: Can I use this skill without a Localhero.ai account?
faq.answer: You can use the skill to understand your existing translation structure and write source strings, but generating translations requires a Localhero.ai account and authentication.

faq.question: What if my project does not use the Localhero CLI yet?
faq.answer: Run `npx @localheroai/cli init` to create a localhero.json configuration file. This sets up your project structure, source locale, and target languages for Localhero.ai.

faq.question: How do I review or edit translations after they are generated?
faq.answer: Use the Localhero.ai web UI at https://localhero.ai. Each PR that triggers the Localhero Action gets its own review page where you can adjust translations before merging.
